alerts.alert: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
alerts.name: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
alerts.desc: Content Security Policy (CSP) is an added layer of security that helps to detect and mitigate certain types of attacks, including Cross Site Scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks. These attacks are used for everything from data theft to site defacement or distribution of malware. CSP provides a set of standard HTTP headers that allow website owners to declare approved sources of content that browsers should be allowed to load on that page — covered types are JavaScript, CSS, HTML frames, fonts, images and embeddable objects such as Java applets, ActiveX, audio and video files.
alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to set the Content-Security-Policy header.

alerts.name: HTTP Only Site
alerts.desc: The site is only served under HTTP and not HTTPS.

instances.otherinfo:
Failed to connect.
ZAP attempted to connect via: https://host.docker.internal:8000

alerts.solution: Configure your web or application server to use SSL (https).
alerts.otherinfo:
Failed to connect.

ZAP attempted to connect via: https://host.docker.internal:8000

alerts.name: Missing Anti-clickjacking Header
alerts.desc: The response does not protect against 'ClickJacking' attacks. It should include either Content-Security-Policy with 'frame-ancestors' directive or X-Frame-Options.
alerts.solution:
Modern Web browsers support the Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options HTTP headers. Ensure one of them is set on all web pages returned by your site/app.

If you expect the page to be framed only by pages on your server (e.g. it's part of a FRAMESET) then you'll want to use SAMEORIGIN, otherwise if you never expect the page to be framed, you should use DENY. Alternatively consider implementing Content Security Policy's "frame-ancestors" directive.

alerts.name: Sub Resource Integrity Attribute Missing
alerts.desc: The integrity attribute is missing on a script or link tag served by an external server. The integrity tag prevents an attacker who have gained access to this server from injecting a malicious content.
alerts.solution: Provide a valid integrity attribute to the tag.

alerts.name: Cookie No HttpOnly Flag
alerts.desc: A cookie has been set without the HttpOnly flag, which means that the cookie can be accessed by JavaScript. If a malicious script can be run on this page then the cookie will be accessible and can be transmitted to another site. If this is a session cookie then session hijacking may be possible.
alerts.solution: Ensure that the HttpOnly flag is set for all cookies.

alerts.name: Cross-Domain JavaScript Source File Inclusion
alerts.desc: The page includes one or more script files from a third-party domain.
alerts.solution: Ensure JavaScript source files are loaded from only trusted sources, and the sources can't be controlled by end users of the application.

alerts.name: Private IP Disclosure
alerts.desc: A private IP (such as 10.x.x.x, 172.x.x.x, 192.168.x.x) or an Amazon EC2 private hostname (for example, ip-10-0-56-78) has been found in the HTTP response body. This information might be helpful for further attacks targeting internal systems.
alerts.solution: Remove the private IP address from the HTTP response body. For comments, use JSP/ASP/PHP comment instead of HTML/JavaScript comment which can be seen by client browsers.

alerts.alert: Server Leaks Information via "X-Powered-By" HTTP Response Header Field(s)
alerts.name: Server Leaks Information via "X-Powered-By" HTTP Response Header Field(s)
alerts.desc: The web/application server is leaking information via one or more "X-Powered-By" HTTP response headers. Access to such information may facilitate attackers identifying other frameworks/components your web application is reliant upon and the vulnerabilities such components may be subject to.
alerts.solution: Ensure that your web server, application server, load balancer, etc. is configured to suppress "X-Powered-By" headers.

alerts.name: Timestamp Disclosure - Unix
alerts.desc: A timestamp was disclosed by the application/web server. - Unix
alerts.solution: Manually confirm that the timestamp data is not sensitive, and that the data cannot be aggregated to disclose exploitable patterns.

alerts.name: X-Content-Type-Options Header Missing
alerts.desc: The Anti-MIME-Sniffing header X-Content-Type-Options was not set to 'nosniff'. This allows older versions of Internet Explorer and Chrome to perform MIME-sniffing on the response body, potentially causing the response body to be interpreted and displayed as a content type other than the declared content type. Current (early 2014) and legacy versions of Firefox will use the declared content type (if one is set), rather than performing MIME-sniffing.

instances.otherinfo:
This issue still applies to error type pages (401, 403, 500, etc.) as those pages are often still affected by injection issues, in which case there is still concern for browsers sniffing pages away from their actual content type.
At "High" threshold this scan rule will not alert on client or server error responses.

This issue still applies to error type pages (401, 403, 500, etc.) as those pages are often still affected by injection issues, in which case there is still concern for browsers sniffing pages away from their actual content type.
At "High" threshold this scan rule will not alert on client or server error responses.

alerts.solution:
Ensure that the application/web server sets the Content-Type header appropriately, and that it sets the X-Content-Type-Options header to 'nosniff' for all web pages.

If possible, ensure that the end user uses a standards-compliant and modern web browser that does not perform MIME-sniffing at all, or that can be directed by the web application/web server to not perform MIME-sniffing.
alerts.otherinfo:
This issue still applies to error type pages (401, 403, 500, etc.) as those pages are often still affected by injection issues, in which case there is still concern for browsers sniffing pages away from their actual content type.

At "High" threshold this scan rule will not alert on client or server error responses.

alerts.name: Modern Web Application
alerts.desc: The application appears to be a modern web application. If you need to explore it automatically then the Ajax Spider may well be more effective than the standard one.

instances.otherinfo: Links have been found that do not have traditional href attributes, which is an indication that this is a modern web application.

alerts.solution: This is an informational alert and so no changes are required.
alerts.otherinfo: Links have been found that do not have traditional href attributes, which is an indication that this is a modern web application.

alerts.name: Session Management Response Identified
alerts.desc: The given response has been identified as containing a session management token. The 'Other Info' field contains a set of header tokens that can be used in the Header Based Session Management Method. If the request is in a context which has a Session Management Method set to "Auto-Detect" then this rule will change the session management to use the tokens identified.
alerts.solution: This is an informational alert rather than a vulnerability and so there is nothing to fix.

alerts.name: User Agent Fuzzer
alerts.desc: Check for differences in response based on fuzzed User Agent (eg. mobile sites, access as a Search Engine Crawler). Compares the response statuscode and the hashcode of the response body with the original response.
